ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03315091 covers dim sum as a food product category. No exact code exists for a mixed steamed-and-fried dim sum set of 5 pieces; closest_parent applied.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers dim sum as a category. The specific mixed set (3 steamed + 2 fried) is not individually coded; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for dim sum or mixed dim sum set; none applied.

main_ingredients: wheat flour dough (har gow, siu mai wrappers)

serving.natural_language_portion: One sharing set of 5 dim sum pieces (3 steamed, 2 fried) intended for 4 persons, estimated at approximately 400 g total (roughly 80 g per piece on average), equivalent to about 100 g per person.

nutrient_source.title: Dim Sum (mixed steamed and fried) — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo entries for har gow, siu mai, and fried wu gok/spring roll, weighted for a 3-steamed-2-fried composition
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo Nutrient Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for ingredient-level cross-check
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry covers a mixed 3-steamed-2-fried dim sum set. Values are estimated as a weighted composite: 60% steamed dim sum (har gow ~100 kcal/100g, siu mai ~120 kcal/100g) and 40% fried dim sum (wu gok/fried taro dumpling ~220 kcal/100g, spring roll ~200 kcal/100g), blended to per-100g basis. Sodium estimated from HPB Foodo dim sum entries (har gow ~380 mg/100g, siu mai ~450 mg/100g, fried ~350 mg/100g). All values are moderate-confidence estimates; exact restaurant recipe not available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of dim sum and Chinese dumplings — analogous estimate based on steamed wheat-flour dumplings (GI ~45–50) and fried wheat-flour pastries (GI ~50–55), weighted composite for 3-steamed-2-fried set
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a mixed dim sum set. Steamed wheat-flour dumplings (har gow, siu mai) have GI approximately 45–50 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed wheat-flour dumplings and wonton. Fried wheat-flour pastries have GI approximately 50–55. Weighted composite (60% steamed GI 47, 40% fried GI 52) yields estimated GI of approximately 48. GL per serving calculated as (48 × 68.8 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 33.1. gi_category set to low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~400 mg/100g; ~1600 mg per full set)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit dim sum consumption or choose steamed varieties with reduced dipping sauce, as the sodium content of a full 4-person set (~1600 mg) can approach or exceed 70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low-to-moderate GI (estimated GI 48)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size, as the full 4-person set provides approximately 69 g of carbohydrate; sharing equally (100 g per person, ~17 g carbohydrate) is manageable within a carbohydrate-controlled meal plan, but the glycemic load of the full set (~33) is significant if consumed by one individual.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork and frying oil, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer the steamed dim sum items over the fried ones, as fried pieces contribute additional saturated fat and total fat; the overall set provides approximately 2.1 g saturated fat per 100 g, and sodium levels are elevated, both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dim Sum Set A (3 Steamed and 2 Fried, 4 Servings) is a traditional Cantonese Chinese sharing platter commonly served in Singapore dim sum restaurants and available via food delivery platforms. The set typically comprises three varieties of steamed dim sum — such as har gow (steamed prawn dumplings), siu mai (open-topped pork and prawn dumplings), and cheung fun (steamed rice noodle rolls) — alongside two fried items such as wu gok (fried taro dumplings) or spring rolls. Steamed pieces are delicate, translucent, and tender with a light umami flavour from pork, prawn, and sesame oil; fried pieces are crispy on the outside with a savoury filling. The set is designed for sharing among four persons, with each person receiving approximately 100 g of food. Nutritionally, the set provides a moderate energy density of approximately 155 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~7.5 g/100g) from pork and shrimp, moderate fat (~6.8 g/100g) elevated by the fried components, and carbohydrate (~17.2 g/100g) from wheat and rice flour wrappers. Sodium content is high (~400 mg/100g), making it a concern for hypertension management. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 48 places it in the low GI category, though the glycemic load of the full set is significant. Clinically, individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size and prefer steamed over fried items where possible. Dim sum is culturally associated with yum cha (morning tea) gatherings and is a staple of Singaporean Chinese cuisine.

Steamed vs fried dim sum calories
Loong Dim Sum Set A nutrition
How much protein in dim sum?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry covers this exact mixed dim sum set from Loong Dim Sum. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite from HPB Singapore Foodo entries for individual steamed and fried dim sum items (har gow, siu mai, wu gok, spring roll), weighted 60% steamed and 40% fried. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed wheat-flour dumplings and fried wheat-flour pastries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-documented in approved sources and the weighting methodology is transparent.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on HPB Foodo entries for individual dim sum items; exact restaurant recipe from Loong Dim Sum - North Canal Road is not available. 2. Vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are reported as -1 because these micronutrients are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo dim sum entries used as the primary source; they are not confirmed absent but are not reported. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 48) derived from University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed wheat-flour dumplings and fried wheat-flour pastries; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific set. 4. The set is priced at SGD 61.80 for 4 persons (~SGD 15.45 per person) at Loong Dim Sum - North Canal Road. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; pork is a primary ingredient, making this unsuitable for halal dietary requirements. 6. The serving size of 400 g total (100 g per person) is an estimate; actual piece weights vary by restaurant. 7. Glycemic load per serving (33.1) is calculated for the full 400 g set; per-person GL is approximately 8.3, which is moderate.
